The **Catholic Church in** **Poland** or **Poland Catholic Church**, is part of the worldwide [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") in [communion](/wiki/Full_communion "Full communion") with the [Pope](/wiki/Pope "Pope") in [Rome](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See"). Established in the 9th century, it has maintained an unbroken communion with the [bishop of Rome](/wiki/Bishop_of_Rome "Bishop of Rome").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/JPII_pomnik_Poznan.jpg/250px-JPII_pomnik_Poznan.jpg)](/wiki/File:JPII_pomnik_Poznan.jpg)

Monument in [Poznań](/wiki/Pozna%C5%84 "Poznań") to Karol Wojtyła, a Pole who was Pope [John Paul II](/wiki/John_Paul_II "John Paul II") from 1978 to 2005.

The Polish [Latin Church](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church") includes 41 [dioceses](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese"). There are three eparchies of the [Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church](/wiki/Ukrainian_Greek_Catholic_Church "Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church") in the country, with members of the [Armenian Catholic Church](/wiki/Armenian_Catholic_Church "Armenian Catholic Church") under the [Ordinariate for Eastern Catholics in Poland](/wiki/Ordinariate_for_Eastern_Catholics_in_Poland "Ordinariate for Eastern Catholics in Poland").[[1]](#cite_note-1) The [ordinaries](/wiki/Ordinary_(church_officer) "Ordinary (church officer)") of these jurisdictions comprise the [Episcopal Conference of Poland](/wiki/Episcopal_Conference_of_Poland "Episcopal Conference of Poland"). Combined, these comprise about 10,000 parishes and religious orders. There are 40.55 million registered Catholics[[2]](#cite_note-statpoland-2)[[3]](#cite_note-iskk-ase2017-3): 4 (the data includes the number of infants baptized) in Poland.[[4]](#cite_note-Wystap.pl-4) The primate of the Church is [Wojciech Polak](/wiki/Wojciech_Polak "Wojciech Polak"), [Archbishop of Gniezno](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Gniezno "Archbishop of Gniezno").

In the early 2000s, 99% of all children born in Poland were baptized Catholic.[[5]](#cite_note-5) In 2015, the church recorded that 97.7% of Poland's population was Catholic.[[2]](#cite_note-statpoland-2) Other statistics suggested this proportion of adherents to Catholicism could be as low as 85%.[[6]](#cite_note-repgus2014-6)[[7]](#cite_note-US2022-7) The rate of decline has been described as "devastating"[[8]](#cite_note-:1-8) the former social prestige and political influence that the Catholic Church in Poland once enjoyed.[[9]](#cite_note-9) On the other hand, a 2023 survey of 36 countries with large Catholic populations using data from the [World Values Survey](/wiki/World_Values_Survey "World Values Survey") revealed that 52% of Polish Catholics claimed to attend Mass weekly, the seventh highest of the nations surveyed and the highest among European countries.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Most Poles adhere to Latin Catholicism. About 71.3% of the population identified themselves as such in the [2021 census](/wiki/2021_Polish_census "2021 Polish census"), down from 88% in [2011](/wiki/2011_Polish_census "2011 Polish census").[[8]](#cite_note-:1-8)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catholic_Church_in_Poland&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Further information: [Christianization of Poland](/wiki/Christianization_of_Poland "Christianization of Poland")

Ever since [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") officially adopted [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") in [966](/wiki/Baptism_of_Poland "Baptism of Poland"), the Catholic Church has played an important religious, cultural and political role in the country post-schism. Identifying oneself as Catholic distinguished Polish culture and nationality from neighbouring [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), especially eastern and northern Germany, which is mostly [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"), and the countries to the east which are [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy"). During times of foreign oppression, the Catholic Church was a cultural guard in the fight for independence and national survival. For instance, the Polish abbey in [Częstochowa](/wiki/Cz%C4%99stochowa "Częstochowa"), which successfully resisted a siege in the [Swedish invasion of Poland](/wiki/Swedish_invasion_of_Poland "Swedish invasion of Poland") in the 17th century, became a symbol of national resistance to the occupation. The establishment of a communist regime controlled by the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") following [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") allowed the Church to continue fulfilling this role, although recent allegations suggest there was some minor collaboration between Polish clergy and the regime.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The 1978 election of Polish Cardinal Karol Wojtyła as [Pope John Paul II](/wiki/Pope_John_Paul_II "Pope John Paul II") strengthened the ties of identification. John Paul's visits to Poland became rallying points for the faithful and galvanized opposition to the Soviet regime.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] His beatification in 2011 and canonization 3 years later further instilled pride and joy in the Polish people.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In 2013, [Pope Francis](/wiki/Pope_Francis "Pope Francis"), John Paul II's 2nd successor (and who was made a cardinal by the Polish pope), announced that [World Youth Day](/wiki/World_Youth_Day "World Youth Day"), the world's largest religious gathering of young people, would be held in [Kraków, Poland](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w,_Poland "Kraków, Poland") in 2016.

In 2013 a succession of [child sex abuse scandals](/wiki/Catholic_Church_sexual_abuse_cases_in_Poland "Catholic Church sexual abuse cases in Poland") within the Church, and the poor response by the Church, became a matter of some public concern.[[12]](#cite_note-telegraph-20140711-12) The church resisted demands to pay compensation to victims.[[13]](#cite_note-ft-20131011-13)

## Number of Catholics in Poland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catholic_Church_in_Poland&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Number of Catholics in Poland")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Uroczysto%C5%9B%C4%87_Naj%C5%9Bwi%C4%99tszego_Cia%C5%82a_i_Krwi_Pa%C5%84skiej_%283617619206%29.jpg/250px-Uroczysto%C5%9B%C4%87_Naj%C5%9Bwi%C4%99tszego_Cia%C5%82a_i_Krwi_Pa%C5%84skiej_%283617619206%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Uroczysto%C5%9B%C4%87_Naj%C5%9Bwi%C4%99tszego_Cia%C5%82a_i_Krwi_Pa%C5%84skiej_(3617619206).jpg)

Procession in [Wrocław](/wiki/Wroc%C5%82aw "Wrocław"), 2009.

As of 2023, a majority of Poles, approximately 71%, identified themselves as Catholic, and 58% said they are active practicing Catholics, according to a survey by the [Centre for Public Opinion Research](/wiki/Centre_for_Public_Opinion_Research_(Poland) "Centre for Public Opinion Research (Poland)").[[14]](#cite_note-14) According to the [Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Poland](/wiki/Ministry_of_Foreign_Affairs_(Poland) "Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Poland)"), 95% of Poles belong to the Catholic Church;[[15]](#cite_note-15) this survey bases the number of adherents on the number of infants baptized,[[4]](#cite_note-Wystap.pl-4) as provided by the Catholic Church. The [CIA Factbook](/wiki/The_World_Factbook "The World Factbook") gives a number of 87.2% belonging to the Catholic Church in 2012.[[16]](#cite_note-16)
In the biggest part of Europe, the rates of religious observance have steadily decreased. However, Poland still remains one of the most devoutly religious countries in Europe. Polish Catholics participate in the [sacraments](/wiki/Sacraments "Sacraments") more frequently than their counterparts in most Western European and North American countries. A 2023 study by the Church itself revealed that 98.5% of Poles go to confession at least once a year, while 97% of the respondents say they do so more often than once a year.[[17]](#cite_note-17) By contrast, a 2005 study by [Georgetown University's](/wiki/Georgetown_University "Georgetown University") Center for Applied Research on the Apostolate revealed that only 14% of [American Catholics](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_the_United_States "Catholic Church in the United States") take part in the [sacrament of penance](/wiki/Sacrament_of_penance "Sacrament of penance") once a year, with a mere 2% doing so more frequently.[[18]](#cite_note-18) [Tarnów](/wiki/Tarn%C3%B3w "Tarnów") is the most religious city in Poland, and [Łódź](/wiki/%C5%81%C3%B3d%C5%BA "Łódź") is the least. The southern and eastern parts of Poland are more active in their religious practices than those of the West and North. The majority of Poles continue to declare themselves Catholic.[[19]](#cite_note-19) This is in stark contrast to the otherwise similar neighboring [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic"), which is one of the [least religious practicing areas on Earth](/wiki/Religion_in_the_Czech_Republic "Religion in the Czech Republic"), with only 35% declaring "they believe there is a God" of any kind.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

A 2014 report by *[L'Osservatore Romano](/wiki/L%27Osservatore_Romano "L'Osservatore Romano")* found that the proportion of Polish Catholics attending Sunday Mass was approximately 39.1%.[[12]](#cite_note-telegraph-20140711-12)[[21]](#cite_note-catholicculture-20140724-21) At the same time, however, this partly results from the fact that since 2004 2.1 million Poles have emigrated to Western Europe.[[21]](#cite_note-catholicculture-20140724-21) Writing for the Catholic weekly [Tygodnik Powszechny](/wiki/Tygodnik_Powszechny "Tygodnik Powszechny"), Church sociologist Rev. Prof. Janusz Mariański has noted that these two million Polish emigrants are still listed in their parish records as members, so when Mass attendance is measured such emigres lower the official records.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The proportion of Mass attendees receiving [Holy Communion](/wiki/Holy_Communion "Holy Communion") is rising, while the number of Polish Catholic priests continues to rise as ordinations outpace deaths in Poland, though the number of nuns is decreasing.

### Religious practice

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catholic_Church_in_Poland&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Religious practice")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/01_Fronleichnamsprozession_in_Sanok_im_Jahr_2012.JPG/250px-01_Fronleichnamsprozession_in_Sanok_im_Jahr_2012.JPG)](/wiki/File:01_Fronleichnamsprozession_in_Sanok_im_Jahr_2012.JPG)

[Corpus Christi](/wiki/Corpus_Christi_(feast) "Corpus Christi (feast)") in Sanok

The [Centre for Public Opinion Research](/wiki/Centre_for_Public_Opinion_Research_(Poland) "Centre for Public Opinion Research (Poland)") regularly conducts surveys on religious practice in Poland. A 2012 document reported that for more than a quarter-century church attendance and declarations of religious faith have been stable, decreasing only minimally since 2005 when the grief related to the death of [Pope John Paul II](/wiki/Pope_John_Paul_II "Pope John Paul II") led to an increase in religious practice among Poles. In a 2012 study, 52% of Poles declared that they attend religious services at least once a week, 38% do so once or twice a month, and 11% do so never or almost never. Meanwhile, 94% of Poles consider themselves to be religious believers (9% of whom consider themselves "deeply religious"), while only 6% of Poles claim that they are non-believers.[[23]](#cite_note-23) According to the Church's own sources,[[3]](#cite_note-iskk-ase2017-3): 4 36.3% of Catholics required to attend the Sunday Mass, take part in it.

[Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter") continues to be an important holiday for Polish Catholics. According to a 2012 study by the CBOS ([Centre for Public Opinion Research](/wiki/Centre_for_Public_Opinion_Research_(Poland) "Centre for Public Opinion Research (Poland)")), 74% of Poles make an effort to participate in the [sacrament of penance](/wiki/Sacrament_of_penance "Sacrament of penance") before Easter, 59% make an effort to attend the [Stations of the Cross](/wiki/Stations_of_the_Cross "Stations of the Cross") or [Gorzkie żale](/wiki/Gorzkie_%C5%BCale "Gorzkie żale") (an increase of 6% since 2003), 57% want to improve themselves for the better (an increase of 7%), 49% want to help the needy (an increase of 8%), and 46% want to pray more .[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Apostasy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catholic_Church_in_Poland&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Apostasy")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section's **factual accuracy is [disputed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute")**. Relevant discussion may be found on the [talk page](/wiki/Talk:Catholic_Church_in_Poland#Disputed "Talk:Catholic Church in Poland"). Please help to ensure that disputed statements are [reliably sourced](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources"). *(November 2020)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

See also: [Apostasy in Christianity](/wiki/Apostasy_in_Christianity "Apostasy in Christianity")

During the [October 2020 Polish protests](/wiki/October_2020_Polish_protests "October 2020 Polish protests"), enquiries regarding the procedure for [apostasy](/wiki/Apostasy "Apostasy") from the Polish Catholic Church became popular. [Web search engine](/wiki/Web_search_engine "Web search engine") queries showed high frequencies for "apostasy" ([Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"): *apostazja*) and "how to do apostasy" ([Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"): *jak dokonać apostazji*), and a [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook") event titled "Quit the church at [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas")" was [followed](/wiki/Friending_and_following "Friending and following") by 5000 people.[[27]](#cite_note-polsat_apostasy-27)

As of 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Catholic_Church_in_Poland&action=edit), the formal apostasy procedure in the Polish Catholic Church is a procedure defined on 7 October 2015 by the [Episcopal Conference of Poland](/wiki/Episcopal_Conference_of_Poland "Episcopal Conference of Poland"), which became effective as of 19 February 2016.[[28]](#cite_note-KEP_2016_apostasy_law-28)[[29]](#cite_note-apostazja_howto-29) It can only be done in person, by delivering an application to a church [parish priest](/wiki/Parish#Catholic_Church "Parish"). The procedure cannot be done by email, post, or state administrative services.[[30]](#cite_note-bezprawnik_apostasy-30)
